questions.question: Explain the step-by-step procedure to create a new Android project in Android Studio.
questions.hint: Remember to mention template selection and SDK configuration
keyPoints: Launch Android Studio and select 'Create New Project'
Choose 'Empty Views Activity' as the project template
Configure project settings: Name, Package name, Save location, Language (Kotlin/Java), Minimum SDK
Click 'Finish' to generate the project structure
Project gets created with default MainActivity and activity_main.xml

questions.question: Describe the purpose of the AndroidManifest.xml file in an Android application.
questions.hint: Think of it as the app's identity card
keyPoints: It is the root configuration file for the Android application
Declares all components of the application (Activities, Services, BroadcastReceivers, ContentProviders)
Specifies required permissions for the app (Internet, Camera, etc.)
Defines the application icon and label
Specifies the minimum and target SDK versions

questions.question: What is the purpose of the activity_main.xml layout file? Where is it located in the project?
questions.hint: Remember it uses XML format and is in the resources folder
keyPoints: Defines the user interface structure of the main activity
Located in res/layout/ directory of the Android project
Contains XML tags representing Views and ViewGroups
Provides visual layout that gets rendered on the device screen
Separates UI design from business logic in Java/Kotlin code

questions.question: Explain the directory structure of a basic Android application created in Android Studio. List the purpose of at least 6 important folders.
questions.hint: Focus on the app/src/main directory structure
keyPoints: app/src/main/java: Contains Java/Kotlin source code files
app/src/main/res: Contains resources like layouts, drawables, values, strings
app/src/main/res/layout: Contains XML layout files for UI
app/src/main/res/values: Contains strings.xml, colors.xml, themes.xml
app/src/main/res/drawable: Contains images and icons
app/build.gradle: Handles project build configuration and dependencies
AndroidManifest.xml: Declares app components and permissions

questions.question: What is an Android Virtual Device (AVD)? Explain how to create and run an app on an AVD.
questions.hint: AVD Manager is accessed from Tools menu in Android Studio
keyPoints: AVD is an emulator configuration that simulates an Android device
Created using AVD Manager in Android Studio (Tools > AVD Manager)
Select device definition, system image, and hardware profile
Launch the emulator from AVD Manager or Run button in Android Studio
App gets installed and runs on the virtual device

questions.question: Explain the code-build-run-debug cycle in Android development with reference to the first Android application.
questions.hint: Focus on the iterative nature of development
keyPoints: CODE: Write application logic in MainActivity.java/Kotlin and design UI in activity_main.xml
BUILD: Click 'Make Project' or 'Run' to compile using Gradle build system
RUN: Deploy the APK to emulator or physical device
DEBUG: Use Logcat and breakpoints in Android Studio to identify issues
Modify code based on testing results and repeat the cycle
onCreate() method is the entry point where the activity initializes

questions.question: What is the role of build.gradle (Module: app) file in an Android project?
keyPoints: Configures build settings for the app module
Declares dependencies (libraries required for the app)
Specifies compileSdkVersion and targetSdkVersion
Defines application ID and version code/version name
Configures signing configurations for debug/release builds

questions.question: Discuss the importance of the MainActivity class in an Android application. Explain the onCreate() method with an example.
questions.hint: Remember to mention setContentView() which connects layout to activity
keyPoints: MainActivity is the entry point of the Android application
It is a subclass of AppCompatActivity (or Activity)
onCreate() is the first lifecycle method called when activity is created
It sets up the UI using setContentView() method
Example: setContentView(R.layout.activity_main) inflates the XML layout
It is where views are initialized and event listeners are set up

questions.question: What is R.java in Android? What is its significance in accessing layout elements?
questions.hint: It is auto-generated - never modify it manually
keyPoints: R.java is an auto-generated file by Android SDK
Located in app/build/generated/ap_generated_sources/debug/out/
Contains resource IDs for all resources in res/ folder
Used to access layout elements programmatically (e.g., R.id.textView)
Acts as a bridge between XML resources and Java/Kotlin code

questions.question: Design a simple Android application to display 'Welcome to Android Development' using TextView. Explain all the necessary files and their modifications.
questions.hint: Focus on XML attributes for TextView configuration
keyPoints: Create new Android project in Android Studio
Modify activity_main.xml: Add TextView with android:text attribute
Code: android:text='Welcome to Android Development'
Set layout_width and layout_height (wrap_content or match_parent)
MainActivity.java: Add findViewById() to get TextView reference if needed
Build and run the app on emulator to see output
Strings.xml: Optionally add string resource for the message

questions.question: List and explain at least 5 essential attributes used in a TextView XML element with their default values.
questions.hint: Consider both layout and text appearance attributes
keyPoints: android:layout_width: Specifies width (wrap_content, match_parent, or dp value)
android:layout_height: Specifies height (wrap_content, match_parent, or dp value)
android:text: Sets the text content displayed
android:textColor: Sets text color (default is black)
android:textSize: Sets text size (default is 14sp)
android:textStyle: Sets text style (normal, bold, italic)
android:padding: Sets internal spacing around text

questions.question: Compare between physical Android device and Android Emulator (AVD) for testing Android applications. Mention at least 4 points of comparison.
questions.hint: Consider performance, functionality, and convenience factors
keyPoints: Physical device: Real hardware performance, Emulator: Depends on computer resources
Physical device: Requires USB debugging enabled, Emulator: No device required
Physical device: Can test hardware features (camera, sensors), Emulator: Limited sensor emulation
Physical device: Faster for quick testing, Emulator: Slower to start and run
Physical device: Shows real-world performance, Emulator: May not reflect exact device behavior
Emulator: Multiple device configurations available, Physical device: Limited to available devices

questions.question: In the Hello World Android app, if you want to change the background color of the layout to light blue and make the TextView text red with size 24sp, write the modified XML code for activity_main.xml.
questions.hint: Remember sp is the unit for text size and hex color codes
keyPoints: Add android:background='#ADD8E6' or android:background='@color/light_blue' to parent layout
Change TextView android:textColor to red: '#FF0000' or '@color/red'
Add android:textSize='24sp' to TextView
Keep android:layout_width and android:layout_height as wrap_content or match_parent
Full XML: TextView with all specified attributes
